Fun fact: Guinea pig teeth never stop growing, so they need to chew on tough fibrous food constantly to keep them worn down to the right length.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Chinchillas come from the cold rocky mountains of South America, where their fur is so thick and dense — about sixty hairs growing from a single follicle — that fleas and other parasites can't even get through it. They don't bathe in water; they roll in special volcanic dust that absorbs oils and keeps their coat perfectly clean. Chinchillas can jump nearly two meters from a standing start. Fun fact: Chinchilla fur is the densest of any land mammal — it's twenty times softer than human hair.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: In the wild, chinchillas live in rocky highland burrows in groups called herds, using their large ears to listen for predators in the thin mountain air. They are crepuscular — most active at dawn and dusk — and spend their activity bursts leaping between rocks, chewing tough plants, and keeping their extraordinary fur in good condition. Chinchillas use a surprising defensive trick: they can release tufts of fur when grabbed, leaving a predator holding a mouthful of fluff while the chinchilla escapes. Fun fact: Chinchillas can live over fifteen years, which is exceptionally long for a small rodent.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Parakeets — sometimes called budgerigars or budgies — are small parrots from the dry grasslands of Australia that have been kept as pets for nearly two hundred years. They can mimic human speech, household sounds, and even ring tones, sometimes picking up words they hear only once. In the wild they fly in enormous flocks of thousands, banking and swirling together like a single living cloud. Fun fact: A parakeet named Puck holds the world record for the largest vocabulary of any bird — he knew over seventeen hundred words.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Wild parakeets are nomadic, following rainfall across Australia and settling wherever grass seeds are ripening. Their green-and-yellow coloring in the wild helps them disappear into sunlit grass. Parakeets tell each other apart using cheek patch patterns and a unique contact call they develop early in life — a kind of name badge in sound. They sleep pressed together on a branch to share warmth. Fun fact: Parakeets can see ultraviolet light, and their feathers reflect UV patterns invisible to human eyes that are used to attract mates.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Cassowaries are the third-tallest birds on Earth and they wear a bony helmet called a casque on top of their head. Nobody is completely sure what it's for — it might help push through thick undergrowth, or amplify the low booming calls they use in the forest. What everyone agrees on is that their middle toe carries a dagger-like claw almost as long as your hand. Fun fact: The cassowary is considered the world's most dangerous bird — it can kick with enough force to break bones.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Cassowaries live in the tropical rainforests of New Guinea and northeastern Australia, mostly alone, eating fallen fruit from the forest floor. They are critical seed dispersers — many rainforest plants depend on cassowaries to eat their seeds and deposit them far away from the parent tree. Fathers incubate the eggs and raise the chicks for about nine months while the mother moves on. Cassowaries can swim strongly and have been spotted crossing wide rivers. Fun fact: The casque on a cassowary's head begins as a small bump and keeps growing throughout the bird's whole life.
encyclopedia.photoAttributionName: PaleoNeolitic (montage creator) BS Thurner Hof Kora27 Martin Sordilla

encyclopedia.shortDescription: The kiwi is a flightless bird from New Zealand that looks a bit like a furry brown rugby ball on legs. Its nostrils sit right at the tip of its long beak — unique among birds — so it sniffs the ground like a dog to find buried worms and grubs in the dark. Kiwi feathers are hair-like and loose, without the stiff interlocking barbs that make most bird feathers smooth. Fun fact: A kiwi egg is so enormous relative to the mother's body that it's roughly the same as a person giving birth to a six-year-old.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Kiwis are nocturnal, sleeping curled up in burrows during the day and foraging through the night. They have tiny vestigial wings hidden under their feathers — too small to see and completely useless for flight. Their bones contain marrow, unlike most birds whose hollow bones make them lighter for flying. Kiwis are deeply territorial and use loud shrieks and hisses to tell neighbours to stay away. Fun fact: Kiwis have some of the most developed nostrils of any bird, and they can smell earthworms underground without touching the soil.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Ostriches are the largest birds alive, and they can sprint at over seventy kilometers per hour — faster than a racehorse at full gallop. They only have two toes on each foot, with one enormous inner toe that acts like a hoof for pushing off the ground. Their long powerful legs can also deliver a kick that can kill a lion. Fun fact: Ostriches have eyes bigger than their brains — and those eyes are the largest of any land animal.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Ostriches live in open African savanna, and their height — up to two and a half meters — gives them an enormous view across the plains. They don't actually bury their head in the sand; they lower their heads to turn their eggs in nest hollows, which from a distance looks like hiding. An ostrich egg is the largest single cell produced by any living animal and takes over an hour to hard-boil. Multiple females lay their eggs in one communal nest, but the dominant female can recognise her own eggs among the others. Fun fact: A single ostrich kick is powerful enough to kill a lion — it's one of the few prey animals that lions are genuinely cautious around.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Emus are big, shaggy, and surprisingly fast — they can hit fifty kilometers per hour on open ground. When emu eggs are laid, the father takes charge: he stops eating and drinking and sits on the nest for eight weeks straight, losing a third of his body weight by the time the chicks hatch. Emus can't fly, but they are strong swimmers and will cross rivers without hesitation. Fun fact: Emus have two sets of eyelids — one for blinking and one to keep out dust and sand.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Emus are nomadic, walking huge distances across Australia following rainfall and fresh plant growth. Their feathers hang in pairs from a single shaft and look more like rough hair than bird plumage, which means wind can ruffle straight through without trapping heat. Emu chicks are striped in black, brown, and cream when they hatch, which camouflages them in grassland while they're too small to run. The male raises the chicks for up to seven months, teaching them what to eat. Fun fact: In 1932 the Australian army actually fought a campaign against wild emus to protect farmland — and the emus won.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Ring-tailed lemurs start each morning by sitting up straight, spreading their arms wide, and basking directly in the sunlight like tiny solar panels warming up for the day. They live in female-led groups called troops and use scent marks to communicate who was where, when, and what mood they were in. Lemurs only live on the island of Madagascar, which split from the African continent millions of years ago and went its own strange way. Fun fact: A lemur troop communicates partly through scent 'stink fights' — males rub their tails on their scent glands and wave them at rivals.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Madagascar has dozens of lemur species, from the tiny mouse lemur the size of a plum to the indri, which sings haunting territorial wails through the forest at dawn. Ring-tailed lemurs are highly social and groom each other constantly to reinforce friendships. Their striped tails are raised high and waved when a troop moves through the forest so everyone can keep track of each other. Fun fact: Lemurs have a special tooth comb — a row of forward-pointing teeth they use for grooming fur and scraping fruit.

Michael Hogan Archaeoindris fontoynonti.jpg: Smokeybjb Microcebus murinus -Artis Zoo, Amsterdam, Netherlands-8a.jpg: Arjan Haverkamp Juvenile Black-and-White Ruffed Lemur, Mantadia, Madagascar.jpg: Frank Vassen Two Brown Lemur, Mantadia, Madagascar.jpg: Frank Vassen derivative work: Maky

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Mandrills are the world's largest monkeys, and the males have brilliant blue-and-red skin on their face and matching coloured patches on their bottom — not paint, just actual skin. The brighter and more intense the colours, the higher that male's rank in the group. They live in huge groups called hordes in Central African rainforests, sometimes with over six hundred individuals moving together through the trees. Fun fact: A mandrill's cheek pouches can hold almost as much food as its stomach.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Mandrills spend their days foraging on the forest floor for fruit, seeds, fungi, insects, and occasionally small animals. Males are much larger than females — nearly twice the weight — and sport the vivid facial markings that females use to assess their strength. Mandrills sleep in trees at night for safety, climbing quickly despite their bulk. They communicate through expressions, postures, and quiet grunts — a full yawn with teeth showing is actually a threat, not tiredness. Fun fact: A mandrill's colourful skin gets brighter when it's excited or dominant — emotions you can literally read from across the forest.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Baboons live in complex family groups called troops that can number into the hundreds. They have a strict social order, and every baboon knows its rank — they keep track through grooming sessions that go on for hours. Baboons will mob and harass even large predators when working as a group, sometimes chasing off leopards. Fun fact: Baboons can walk up to twelve kilometers a day searching for food across open grasslands.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Baboons are highly adaptable — they live in savanna, woodland, mountains, and even semi-desert, eating just about anything they can find: grass, tubers, insects, fruit, eggs, and occasionally small animals. Older females in a troop are often the most important individuals for group decision-making — they know where water is, where dangers lurk, and when to move on. Baby baboons cling to their mothers' bellies in the early weeks, then later ride on their backs like tiny jockeys. Fun fact: Baboons post sentinels — individuals who watch from high points and bark specific alarm calls depending on which predator they've spotted.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Orangutans are the only great apes that live mostly alone instead of in groups, and they spend almost their entire lives up in the forest canopy. Every evening they build a new sleeping platform from bent branches and leaves — a fresh, comfortable nest ten to thirty meters off the ground. They have the longest childhood of any non-human animal, staying with and learning from their mothers for up to eight years. Fun fact: Orangutans have been observed making raincoats out of large leaves during downpours, holding them above their heads.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Orangutans are creative problem-solvers — they've been seen using sticks as tools to fish insects from bark, using leaves as cups to drink water, and even crafting gloves from leaves when handling spiny fruit. Their long, hook-like fingers are perfect for swinging from branch to branch in a motion called brachiating — they can travel through the canopy without ever touching the ground. Males develop large cheek pads called flanges and make a long-call that can carry over a kilometer through dense forest. Fun fact: Orangutans share about ninety-seven percent of their DNA with humans — they're our third-closest relatives after chimpanzees and bonobos.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Chimpanzees are the only animals besides humans that have been seen making and using tools in the wild — stripping leaves off twigs to fish termites from mounds, cracking nuts with specific stones they carry between sites, and using leaves as sponges to soak up water from tree hollows. They live in communities that can have over a hundred individuals and have fierce rivalries with neighbouring groups. Fun fact: Chimpanzees can recognise themselves in a mirror, which is something most animals simply cannot do.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Chimpanzees communicate with a rich vocabulary of hoots, screams, pants, and grunts that other chimps can interpret from far away. Their 'pant-hoot' call — a rising series of calls that ends in a scream — is a personal signature and can be used to locate specific individuals across the forest. They share up to ninety-eight-point-seven percent of DNA with humans. Mothers teach their young for up to ten years, passing on specific tool techniques, food sources, and local traditions — things that vary between chimp communities the way cultures vary between human groups. Fun fact: Chimpanzees remember the faces of individuals they haven't seen in over twenty years.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Bonobos look almost identical to chimpanzees but they live south of the Congo River, which has separated their two lineages for two million years. Bonobo groups are led by females, and they resolve conflict and tension through social bonding rather than fighting — which keeps their communities remarkably calm compared to other apes. They are one of our two closest living relatives, tied with chimpanzees. Fun fact: Bonobos are the only great apes known to look directly into a stranger's eyes as a form of greeting rather than a threat.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Bonobos live in the dense rainforests of the Democratic Republic of Congo — a range so difficult to access that scientists spent decades studying them only in zoos before getting a real look at wild behaviour. They share food willingly with strangers, even when they don't have to — something uncommon in the animal world. Young bonobos play constantly and stay in close contact with their mothers for years. Like chimpanzees, they use tools and can learn to communicate using symbols when working with researchers. Fun fact: Female bonobos form strong alliances that control group decisions — even large males defer to senior females.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Gibbons are the fastest tree-moving primates on Earth, swinging between branches in a motion called brachiation — using just the momentum from one swing to throw themselves to the next branch without stopping. They have arms so long they drag on the ground when walking upright. Gibbons pair up and sing duets at dawn — a rising, whooping call that echoes through the forest and tells other gibbons exactly where they are and that this territory is taken. Fun fact: A gibbon can travel up to fourteen meters in a single swing without slowing down.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Gibbons are small apes — not monkeys — and they're the most acrobatic of all primates, reaching speeds of over fifty kilometers per hour through the canopy in full swing. They're monogamous, living in tight family units with one pair and their offspring, and they spend hours each morning singing together to reinforce their bond and warn neighbours away. Unlike the great apes, gibbons have a ball-and-socket wrist joint that rotates freely, making their swooping swings smooth and efficient. Fun fact: Gibbons can walk upright on two legs along branches, using their long arms for balance like a tightrope walker.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Tapirs have a short, flexible snout that they use like a tiny elephant trunk — perfect for grabbing leaves and pulling fruit off branches. They look a bit like a pig crossed with a rhinoceros, but they're actually most closely related to horses and rhinos. Tapirs are excellent swimmers and love to wade in rivers and lakes to cool down and escape insects. Fun fact: Baby tapirs are born with spotted-and-striped coats that make them almost invisible on the dappled rainforest floor.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Tapirs are living fossils — their body shape has barely changed in twenty million years. There are five living species, found in Central and South America and Southeast Asia, all of them shy forest animals that mostly come out at night. They are important seed dispersers, eating fruit and depositing seeds in new locations through their dung — essentially gardening the forest without knowing it. Tapirs use scent to mark territory and communicate, and they recognise each other partly by smell. Fun fact: A tapir can hold its breath and walk along the bottom of a river to feed on aquatic plants.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Wombats are stocky, powerful Australian marsupials that dig elaborate burrow systems with their strong claws and flattened head. Their pouch opens backward — facing the tail rather than the head — so dirt doesn't pour in while they're digging. When a predator chases them into a burrow, wombats use their thick, tough bottom to block the entrance like a living trapdoor — the predator simply cannot get past. Fun fact: Wombats are the only animals in the world known to produce cube-shaped droppings.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: A wombat burrow can be over thirty meters long and last for generations, with multiple rooms and several entrance tunnels. Wombats are surprisingly fast for their build — they can sprint at forty kilometers per hour over short distances. They're nocturnal grazers, coming out to eat grass and roots before retreating underground at dawn. A single young wombat spends six to seven months in the pouch, then another year following its mother. Fun fact: Wombats mark their territory with those distinctive cubic droppings, leaving them on rocks and logs where they won't roll away.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Tasmanian devils are the size of a small dog but have the most powerful bite relative to body size of any living mammal — they're such thorough eaters that they can finish an entire meal, leaving almost nothing behind. They get their name from the blood-curdling screeches, growls, and screams they make while competing over food, which can be heard from a kilometre away at night. Fun fact: Tasmanian devils glow blue-green under ultraviolet light — nobody is completely sure why.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Tasmanian devils are marsupials, now living only on the island of Tasmania after disappearing from mainland Australia thousands of years ago. They are scavengers as much as hunters, cleaning up carcasses with extreme thoroughness. Babies are born tiny — smaller than a grain of rice — and the mother only has four teats but may give birth to dozens of joeys, so only the first four to reach the pouch survive. Tasmanian devil society is noisy and dramatic but not territorial — individuals wander widely and meet only to eat and breed. Fun fact: Tasmanian devils can eat up to forty percent of their own body weight in a single meal and then sleep it off for the rest of the day.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: The aardvark has a pig-like snout, long rabbit-like ears, and a body that looks like it couldn't quite decide what it wanted to be — but it is perfectly designed for digging into rock-hard termite mounds and slurping up thousands of insects a night. Its sticky tongue is up to thirty centimeters long and moves in and out of its mouth up to a hundred times a minute. Aardvarks are so important to the ecosystem that other animals move into their abandoned burrows — warthogs, hyenas, foxes, and owls all use old aardvark holes. Fun fact: The aardvark is the only living member of its entire order — there is nothing quite like it anywhere else on Earth.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Aardvarks are nocturnal, emerging after dark and walking up to thirty kilometers a night following their nose from one termite mound to the next. Their claws are so strong they can break open concrete-hard termite towers in under a minute. They have poor eyesight but excellent hearing — those tall, independently rotating ears can swivel toward the smallest sound. Aardvarks seal their nostrils with special muscles while digging so no dust gets in. When threatened, they dig straight down at shocking speed or curl into a defensive ball and lash out with their claws. Fun fact: An aardvark's teeth have no enamel — instead they are made of tightly packed tubes of dentine and keep regrowing throughout its life.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Numbats are tiny marsupials from Western Australia with rust-coloured fur and white stripes across their backs — perfect for vanishing into dappled forest shadow. Unlike most marsupials, they're out in full daylight, nose-down in rotting logs hunting termites. Fun fact: A numbat can flick its long sticky tongue in and out of a termite tunnel up to a hundred times per minute.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: The numbat doesn't bite or chew — it laps up termites with a tongue nearly as long as its head. It needs to eat around twenty thousand termites every single day just to stay healthy, so it spends almost every waking moment searching hollow logs and crumbly soil. Numbats have no pouch; the joeys cling to the mother's belly fur until they're old enough to be left in a burrow. Fun fact: Numbats are one of the only marsupials that are strictly diurnal — active by day and asleep by night — because their termite prey is warmest and most active when the sun is out.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Fennec foxes are the tiniest wild dogs on Earth, but their ears are comically enormous — almost as long as their whole face — and they can swivel each ear separately to pinpoint a beetle rustling under the sand. They live in the Sahara and don't need to drink water at all, getting everything they need from food. Fun fact: A fennec fox's huge ears are lined with blood vessels that release heat and keep the animal cool in temperatures that would cook most creatures.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Fennec foxes dig burrows in the sand that can have up to fifteen entrances, making a whole underground maze to escape predators. Their thick paw pads act like oven mitts on sand hot enough to fry an egg. At night they come alive, chasing insects, lizards, and small birds with quick zigzag leaps. They communicate with yips, barks, and a chattering sound called a 'gekkering' that sounds like a tiny argument. Fun fact: Fennec foxes have the largest ears relative to body size of any dog family member in the world.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Servals have the longest legs of any cat relative to their body size, and they use those springs to rocket straight up and bat birds clean out of the air. Their enormous oval ears can rotate independently and pick up the rustle of a mouse moving underground — then the serval stamps a front paw right on the spot to flush it out. Fun fact: A serval's hunting strike is so fast it hits its target in under one tenth of a second.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Servals hunt in the tall grass of African wetlands and savannas, moving slowly with ears tipped forward, listening more than looking. When they detect prey, they freeze, calculate — then leap as high as three metres straight up, twisting in midair to land on target. They eat mostly rodents and birds, and they succeed on about half their pounces, which is an unusually high score for a wild cat. Fun fact: Servals have more than thirty different vocalisations, including a high-pitched call mothers use to find lost cubs in dense reed beds.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Caracals are medium-sized wild cats with sleek sandy coats and dramatic black ear tufts that look like they're wearing fancy hats. They're famous for leaping high into the air and batting multiple birds out of a flock in one lightning-fast burst — sometimes catching two at once. Fun fact: The word 'caracal' comes from a Turkish phrase meaning 'black ears.'
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Caracals live across Africa, the Middle East, and parts of South Asia in grasslands, scrublands, and open woodland. They're extremely secretive and mostly hunt at night, stalking close before exploding into a sprint. A caracal can jump over two metres straight up and use its big front paws like paddles to swat birds mid-flight. They're strong enough to drag prey heavier than themselves into a tree, just like a small leopard. Fun fact: Caracals can rotate each ear tufted ear independently through a full one hundred and eighty degrees, helping them triangulate the exact location of sounds.
encyclopedia.photoAttributionName: Charles Barilleaux from Cincinnati, Ohio, United States of America

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Lynxes have chunky, oversized paws that spread wide and act like built-in snowshoes, letting them walk right across deep snow that a deer would sink into. Their short spotted tail has a black tip, and their cheek fur puffs out like a full beard in winter. Fun fact: A Canada lynx and the snowshoe hare it hunts go through population booms and crashes together — when hares crash, lynx numbers crash shortly after.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Lynx are ambush hunters, crouching motionless in snow-covered brush for long stretches, then erupting in a short explosive charge. Their eyesight is so sharp they can spot a mouse at the distance of a soccer pitch. The Eurasian lynx is the biggest, large enough to bring down a young deer alone. All species have tufted ears that are thought to help funnel sound into the ear canal. Fun fact: Lynx paws can spread to the width of a human hand, distributing the animal's weight so evenly it can cross powdery snow without sinking.
encyclopedia.photoAttributionName: Jan Czeczotka (c)"Programa de Conservación Ex-situ del Lince Ibérico www.lynxexsitu.es" Denali National Park and Preserve Becker1999 (Paul and Cathy)

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Bobcats are the most common wild cat in North America, but most people never see one — they're quiet, cautious, and very good at staying invisible in forests, swamps, and even the edges of towns. Their stubby 'bobbed' tail is where the name comes from. Fun fact: A bobcat can leap three metres in a single bound when it makes its final pounce.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Bobcats are twice the size of a house cat but built for wilderness life — thick spotted fur, long legs, and tufted ears that swivel toward the faintest sound. They hunt rabbits, squirrels, birds, and sometimes deer, usually at dawn and dusk. A bobcat's territory can be hundreds of square kilometres, and it patrols the borders constantly, leaving scent marks on rocks and logs. They're surprisingly good climbers and sometimes rest in tree forks to survey the ground below. Fun fact: Bobcats are so adaptable they've been found living at the edges of cities, raiding chicken coops and hunting rats in overgrown lots.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Ocelots are medium-sized wild cats with coats covered in gorgeous dark-edged rosettes and streaks — each animal's pattern is totally unique. They live in the forests of Central and South America and are almost entirely nocturnal, slipping through the dark so smoothly that local people call them 'little jaguars.' Fun fact: Ocelots have rough tongues powerful enough to strip feathers and fur clean off their prey.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Ocelots are the acrobats of the cat world — they can turn their back feet inward so their rear paws point backwards, letting them climb straight down a tree trunk head-first the way a squirrel does. They swim well and don't avoid water the way most cats do. Ocelots hunt everything from iguanas and crabs to monkeys and deer, locating prey mostly through smell and hearing in total darkness. Fun fact: An ocelot's eyes have more rods than a human eye, making its night vision so good it can navigate dense rainforest under starlight.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Marmots are large, chubby ground squirrels that live in mountain meadows and use piercing whistles to warn the whole colony the moment they spot a hawk or fox. When the whistle goes, every marmot dives for a burrow entrance at once. Fun fact: Marmots hibernate for up to eight months of the year — the longest hibernation of any mammal their size.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Marmots spend the short mountain summer eating almost non-stop, doubling their body weight in fat before retreating underground for winter. During hibernation their heart rate drops from around a hundred beats per minute to just five, and their body temperature falls to just above freezing. Different species live on different continents — there are marmots in the Alps, the Rockies, and the Himalayas. Their burrows can go two metres underground with separate rooms for sleeping and bathroom use. Fun fact: A marmot can lose more than a third of its body weight during a single winter's hibernation without any lasting harm.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Prairie dogs are burrowing squirrels that build massive underground towns on the grasslands of North America — one historic 'town' stretched over twenty-five thousand square kilometres. They communicate with a series of barks and chirps that researchers think actually describe intruders in detail: size, colour, and how fast it's moving. Fun fact: Prairie dog towns support dozens of other species, from burrowing owls who move into empty tunnels to black-footed ferrets who hunt right through the corridors.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: A prairie dog colony is a city with social structure — family groups called coteries each defend a territory within the town, and members greet each other with a nuzzle that looks almost like a kiss. When a hawk appears, one prairie dog gives a specific bark and the whole town drops underground within seconds. They clip the grass around burrow entrances short, giving themselves a clear view of approaching danger. Fun fact: Scientists studying prairie dog calls have found evidence that they communicate different descriptions for different predators — changing their call depending on whether the threat is a coyote, a hawk, or a human.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Yaks live higher than almost any other large mammal on Earth — up on the Tibetan plateau where the air is so thin that most animals would collapse. Their blood has extra-large red blood cells that soak up oxygen much more efficiently than ours do. Fun fact: Wild yaks can weigh as much as a small car — over a thousand kilograms.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Yaks have three layers of fur: coarse outer hairs, a woolly middle layer, and a dense inner fleece so warm it lets them sleep on snow without losing body heat. They use their broad hooves and strong necks to sweep snow aside and reach the frozen grass underneath. Yak milk is thick and pinkish, and nomadic herders on the Tibetan plateau rely on yaks for milk, wool, transport, and fuel — dried yak dung burns well at altitude where there's no wood. Fun fact: Yaks communicate mostly through grunt-like grunts — they almost never moo the way cows do, which is why their scientific name literally means 'grunting ox.'

encyclopedia.shortDescription: The saiga antelope has a large, droopy, almost trunk-like nose that looks like no other animal's face. That strange nose is a superpower: in summer it filters out the clouds of dust kicked up by stampeding herds, and in winter it warms and humidifies freezing air before it reaches the lungs. Fun fact: Saiga can sprint at over eighty kilometres per hour across the open steppe.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Saigas are among the most ancient large mammals alive, having shared grasslands with woolly mammoths and sabre-toothed cats. They migrate in enormous herds across the grasslands of Central Asia, travelling hundreds of kilometres between seasonal feeding grounds. Their numbers crashed dramatically in recent decades, then began recovering — showing that conservation efforts can work even for wild herd animals. Fun fact: Saiga antelope calves can stand up and run within minutes of being born, because on open steppe there is nowhere to hide and everything depends on speed.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: When wolves approach a musk ox herd, the adults don't run — they form a tight circle with calves in the centre, each adult facing outward with horns lowered, daring anything to try its luck. Their thick double-layered coats are so good at holding heat that the animals can stand perfectly still in blizzards. Fun fact: The soft undercoat of a musk ox, called qiviut, is one of the warmest natural fibres on Earth — warmer than sheep's wool by a factor of eight.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Musk oxen have lived in the Arctic for thousands of years, outlasting the woolly mammoth and surviving some of the most brutal winters on the planet. Their hooves have sharp edges that grip ice and scoop through snow to reach buried grasses underneath. Despite the name, it's actually the bulls that produce a strong musky scent during mating season — not the whole species year-round. Fun fact: A musk ox's outer guard hairs can grow to almost seventy centimetres long, sweeping the ground and forming a full curtain of insulation around the animal.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Ibex are wild mountain goats that walk on near-vertical cliff faces as casually as you'd stroll across a flat pavement. Their hooves have hard outer edges that grip rock and soft rubbery inner pads that grip smooth stone — essentially a built-in climbing shoe. Fun fact: Male ibex can grow curved horns over a metre long, and they use them to clash head-on in battles that can be heard echoing across whole valleys.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Ibex live in mountain ranges across Europe, Africa, and Asia, and each morning they climb higher as the day warms, grazing as they go. In winter they descend to avoid the deepest snow. Males — bucks — lock horns and rear up on their back legs to slam together in slow-motion wrestling matches during the autumn rut. Despite looking precarious, ibex almost never fall — their split hooves and low centre of gravity give them extraordinary balance. Fun fact: Alpine ibex were hunted nearly to extinction in the 1800s, but a careful breeding programme in Italy rebuilt the population from just a few dozen animals.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Mountain goats are not actually true goats — they're more closely related to antelopes — but they live on cliffs so steep that no predator bothers to follow them. Their hoof pads are almost rubbery, gripping rock the way a climbing shoe grips a wall, and a notch in each hoof acts like a brake to stop them sliding backwards. Fun fact: Mountain goats can stand on a ledge the width of a credit card without flinching.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Mountain goats live in the Rocky Mountains and along the Pacific coast ranges, spending their lives above the tree line where the air is thin and the terrain is brutal. Their shaggy white coats are so effective at insulation that snow settles on their backs without melting. Kids — the babies — can climb within hours of birth, and they spend their first days scrambling across rocks while their mothers graze nearby. Fun fact: Mountain goats shed their whole thick winter coat in big dangling chunks every spring, often leaving tufts of white wool snagged on bushes and rocks.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Capybaras are the largest rodents on Earth — basically a guinea pig the size of a Labrador — and they are so calm and sociable that birds, monkeys, and even caimans have been photographed sitting on them unbothered. They love water, spend most hot afternoons half-submerged, and can hold their breath for five minutes. Fun fact: Capybaras have slightly webbed feet that make them surprisingly fast swimmers.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Capybaras live in groups of ten to thirty along river banks and around lakes in South America. Their eyes, ears, and nostrils are all set high on their heads so they can watch for danger while almost completely submerged. They eat mostly grass, communicating with soft purrs, clicks, and barks. Groups have a dominant male who keeps order with scent marking, and every member takes turns watching the water's edge for caimans. Fun fact: Capybaras practice a behaviour called autocoprophagy — they eat their own droppings in the morning because the partially digested grass inside is rich in protein and gut bacteria they need.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: The cougar holds the world record for the animal with the most common names — puma, mountain lion, panther, catamount, and over forty others — and it lives in more places than any other wild cat in the Western Hemisphere, from Yukon to Patagonia. It can leap five metres straight up from a standing start. Fun fact: Cougars can't roar — instead they purr like a house cat, and females make a piercing scream during mating season that sounds almost human.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Cougars are solitary ambush hunters, following prey for long distances before getting close enough to sprint. They're powerful enough to bring down an elk far larger than themselves, using a precise bite to the neck to make the hunt as quick as possible. After a big meal, a cougar covers the carcass with leaves and dirt and returns to eat over several days. They avoid humans almost completely, which is why, despite living near cities, they're almost never seen. Fun fact: A cougar's hind legs are longer than its front legs, giving it enormous spring power for leaping — they regularly jump from the ground onto ledges three metres high.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Mongooses are famous for fighting cobras — and winning — because they're fast enough to dodge the strike and thick-furred enough that fangs often can't penetrate the skin. They have a partial resistance to snake venom, so even a bite isn't always a death sentence. Fun fact: Meerkats are a type of mongoose — the same fast-reflexed, sharp-eyed family.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Mongooses live across Africa, southern Asia, and southern Europe in grasslands, forests, and rocky scrub. Some species live alone; others, like the banded mongoose, live in close-knit groups that groom each other, babysit each other's young, and mob predators together. They eat snakes, scorpions, beetles, eggs, and fruit — practically anything they can get their sharp little teeth around. A mongoose attacking a cobra is all about speed: it feints, dodges, and then bites the back of the head before the snake can recover. Fun fact: Mongooses have specialised acetylcholine receptors that give them a built-in partial resistance to snake venom.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Civets are cat-like mammals from Africa and Asia with spotted coats, ringed tails, and very active scent glands — they smear a thick musky paste called civetone onto branches and rocks to mark their territory. The world's most expensive coffee is made from beans that have passed through a civet's digestive system. Fun fact: Civets are not cats at all — they're more closely related to mongooses and hyenas.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Civets come out at night, moving through forest and scrubland with their nose close to the ground, hunting insects, small animals, and fruit. Their scent glands produce a waxy secretion that has been used in perfume-making for centuries. Palm civets in Asia often raid orchards, eating ripe fruit and leaving seeds in their droppings — making them important seed dispersers in forest ecosystems. Fun fact: African civet scent was one of the original ingredients in classic perfumes because it has a complex smell that blends with and fixes other fragrances.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Dugongs are the sea cows of the Indian Ocean and Pacific, grazing on underwater meadows of seagrass the way a cow grazes on a field. Ancient sailors who spotted them from a distance thought they were mermaids — and you can sort of see why, from far away with bad eyesight. Fun fact: Dugongs are the only marine mammals that eat almost nothing but plants.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: A dugong ploughs through seagrass beds using its broad downturned snout, leaving long trenches in the sandy seafloor as it uproots whole plants. They breathe at the surface every few minutes, making barely a ripple. Dugongs live for up to seventy years, which means a slow reproductive rate — females only have a calf once every three to seven years. They're closely related to manatees and distantly to elephants, which makes more sense when you look at a dugong's face up close. Fun fact: Dugongs communicate with chirps, whistles, and squeaks underwater, and a calf stays close to its mother's side for nearly two years.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Narwhals are medium-sized whales that live in Arctic waters — and males grow a single spiralling tusk that can be up to three metres long, sticking out of the left side of their upper jaw. For centuries nobody outside the Arctic knew what creature the tusks came from, and they were sold as genuine unicorn horns. Fun fact: The narwhal tusk is actually a tooth — a canine that grows outward through the lip and keeps spiralling for the whale's whole life.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Narwhals spend winter under pack ice, diving to almost two kilometres deep to hunt Arctic cod and Greenland halibut. They navigate and communicate using clicks and squeals, which bounce off the ice and seafloor to build a picture of the world beneath the water. Groups called pods surface together through cracks in the ice, turning the narrow leads into a flicker of grey and tusk. The tusk is lined with millions of nerve endings — scientists think narwhals can sense temperature, salinity, and pressure through it. Fun fact: Narwhal tusks always spiral in the same direction — counter-clockwise — on every single male ever examined.
encyclopedia.photoAttributionName: Dr. Kristin Laidre, Polar Science Center, UW NOAA/OAR/OER

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Raccoon dogs are the only wild dogs that hibernate — not deeply, but enough to sleep through the coldest winter weeks curled up in a den, living off their fat reserves. Their raccoon-like face mask is just a coincidence: they're actually canids, more closely related to foxes than to raccoons. Fun fact: Raccoon dogs are the only dog family members that regularly climb trees.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Raccoon dogs live in forests and wetlands across East Asia, and populations have spread into Europe. They're omnivores — eating frogs, fish, berries, insects, and pretty much whatever turns up — which makes them adaptable and hard to outcompete. Unlike most dogs, they're quiet animals, using soft whimpers and mews rather than barking. Pairs mate for life and raise pups together, with both parents sharing feeding duties. Fun fact: Raccoon dogs fatten up so aggressively before winter that they can nearly double their body weight in summer, then lose it all slowly through their winter rest.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Sun bears live in the rainforests of Southeast Asia and are the smallest bear species — roughly the size of a large dog. They have jet-black fur with a pale crescent chest patch that gives them their name, and an extraordinarily long tongue — up to twenty-five centimetres — that they use to extract honey from beehives and termites from mounds. Fun fact: Sun bears have the longest tongue relative to body size of any bear.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Sun bears are built for tree life: curved claws grip bark like grappling hooks, and their short, sleek fur stays clean of parasites as they move through dense jungle. They use their claws to rip open dead logs and their long flexible tongue to lap up everything inside. Sun bears are largely solitary and mostly nocturnal in areas where humans are active, but in safe forests they move at any hour. They play a huge role in forest ecosystems by breaking open dead wood and spreading seeds. Fun fact: Sun bears have exceptionally wrinkled faces and unusually long, mobile tongues — the wrinkling may help them make complex facial expressions that communicate with other bears.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Spectacled bears are the only bears native to South America, living in the misty cloud forests of the Andes. They get their name from the pale rings of fur around their eyes that look like spectacles — no two bears have the same pattern. Fun fact: Spectacled bears are the real-life inspiration for Paddington Bear, who comes from 'darkest Peru.'
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Spectacled bears are excellent climbers and spend more time in trees than any other bear species, sometimes sleeping on platforms of broken branches they build themselves high in the canopy. They eat mostly plant material — bromeliads, cactus flowers, fruit, and palm hearts — but will eat insects and small animals when they can catch them. They're shy animals that avoid people and are rarely seen. Loss of cloud forest habitat is the main reason their numbers are falling. Fun fact: Spectacled bears can climb to over four thousand metres altitude in the Andes, making them the highest-living bear species on Earth.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Proboscis monkeys live in the mangrove forests of Borneo, and the males grow enormous drooping noses that hang down over their mouths. When a male honks to attract a mate or warn rivals, that big nose amplifies the sound like a built-in megaphone. Fun fact: Proboscis monkeys have partially webbed feet and are strong swimmers — they regularly cross rivers and have even been seen swimming in the ocean.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Proboscis monkeys live in multi-male groups in the trees along riverbanks, sleeping close together at night and leaping into the water without hesitation when threatened. Their pot-bellied stomachs contain multiple chambers like a cow's, full of specialised bacteria that ferment and digest the leaves and unripe fruit they eat. Fermented food causes gases to build up — which makes their round bellies very distinctive and sometimes very noisy. Fun fact: A male proboscis monkey's nose continues growing throughout his entire life, and older males have the most impressively pendulous noses of all.

encyclopedia.shortDescription: Howler monkeys start every morning with a chorus of deep, rolling roars that carry through the rainforest for up to five kilometres — long before the sun has cleared the treetops. They live in family troops and roar together to tell neighbouring groups where they are, so everyone stays out of each other's way. Fun fact: Howler monkeys have a specially enlarged hyoid bone in their throat — a hollow resonating chamber that turns a medium-sized monkey into a sound system.
encyclopedia.fullExtract: Howler monkeys spend most of their time sitting still in the canopy, eating leaves and waiting for the leaves to digest. Leaves are hard to break down and not very nutritious, so howlers conserve energy at every opportunity — moving slowly, resting often, and letting their loud calls do the work that other monkeys would use up energy travelling to settle. A howler's prehensile tail grips branches like a fifth hand, leaving the arms free to reach for food. Troops usually howl at dawn and dusk, but any disturbance can set off a round of calls that ripples through the whole forest. Fun fact: The howler monkey's roar can be heard up to five kilometres away through dense rainforest — made possible by a hollow bone in the throat that works like a natural amplifier.
